title: Meridian Accord of Ironquay
content:
The Meridian Accord of Ironquay (1850) was a diplomatic agreement in which Freya Quillen, peace delegate of Commonwealth of Tallmere, helped finalize terms at Rivermark.

Implementation.

Commission reports from 1851 show gradual demobilization and the reopening of overland routes through the Rivermark basin.

Background.

By the early 1850s, diplomats from Commonwealth of Tallmere sought a settlement after years of border incidents near Rivermark.

Reactions.

Newspapers in Commonwealth of Tallmere praised the compromise, while opposition leaders argued that naval access points near Rivermark were not sufficiently protected.

Historical assessment.

Modern historians view the Meridian Accord of Ironquay as a stabilizing milestone, crediting Freya Quillen's insistence on verifiable inspection clauses.

Primary sources.

Archived minutes from the Rivermark conference hall include Freya Quillen's opening address and the ratification roll call.

Negotiations.

Freya Quillen, serving as peace delegate, chaired preliminary talks that began in spring 1849. Neutral observers from neighboring cantons attended the sessions.

Signing and terms.

The Meridian Accord of Ironquay was signed in Rivermark on 14 September 1850. The agreement established trade corridors and a joint commission chaired initially by Freya Quillen.
